frame_description: AI agents reduce the labor and friction required to write or assemble software.
representative_claim: I went from not having written code in twenty years to coding all the time now—through agents.

frame_description: Cheap model tokens can be substituted for scarce human time when outputs can be checked.
representative_claim: I’ll throw Codex, Claude, and Gemini at the same problem over and over and just waste tokens to save time.

frame_description: Engineers increasingly build agentic software factories that produce multiple outputs.
representative_claim: Or it may not even be software with a human front end—it might just be completely agentic, an agent accessing it, and you talk to it through voice to control hardware.

frame_description: Agents reduce the cost of experimentation, debugging, and repeated iteration.
representative_claim: And the third-order effect is you can get rid of the not-very-great engineers and have a small number of really creative ones who can iterate rapidly, because the cost of change goes down.

frame_description: AI makes producing information, plans, and candidate solutions cheaper.
representative_claim: The thing AI hasn’t come to yet, but I think it will within the next year—probably within 2026—and that will be very exciting: right now it can generate software, but soon it will generate STEP files and PCB layouts.

frame_description: Agents take over coordination and execution tasks formerly routed through people.
representative_claim: AI provided base-level intelligence and domain knowledge and cut through the jargon; now agents provide a lot of agency.

frame_description: Choosing the right problem matters more than raw implementation speed.
representative_claim: Not to even mention—if you choose the right thing to work on versus the wrong thing to work on, that’s an infinity difference.

frame_description: Human judgment and taste determine which agent outputs and tradeoffs are acceptable.
representative_claim: They’ll give you a really good trade-offs matrix.

frame_description: Architecture and technology selection remain high-leverage human decisions.
representative_claim: Choosing between Postgres versus some other database.

frame_description: Cheap generation increases the importance of verification and final-output evaluation.
representative_claim:
I’m signing off on understanding the consequences.” Or, “I wrote the test harness, the simulations, the proofs, the type-checkers—even without reading this, I have confidence I can sign off that it’ll be safe in production.”

There’s a world in which we embrace that everything is going to be spaghetti code we don’t fully understand, but we write the evaluators that give us confidence, and we rely on people—the infrastructure production engineers—to say, “Okay, I’m fine sending this into prod.” Someone is going to get paged if your systems go down.

frame_description: Responsibility for consequences cannot be delegated merely because execution is automated.
representative_claim: We were responsible, we did clinical trials, we reported all the data, but —

frame_description: Domain knowledge changes the quality of feedback supplied to models.
representative_claim: As long as we have verifiable domains and solved problems, they’re going to resolve those problems.

frame_description: Credibility and truthful communication remain scarce commercial assets.
representative_claim: I just try to figure things out, and then if there’s something I believe in, I try to convey that as accurately and honestly as possible to the other person.

frame_description: AI can amplify already exceptional engineers and founders more than average users.
representative_claim: If someone’s really good with AI and really smart and creative, I want to hire them more than ever, for the leverage.

frame_description: Junior and expert users may receive different leverage because feedback quality differs.
representative_claim: But doesn’t an experienced architect get 10x where a junior engineer gets 2x?

frame_description: AI lowers expertise barriers and increases the reach of capable generalists.
representative_claim: That barrier going down means generalists are having a field day.

frame_description: AI lowers the number of people required for a task and enables smaller teams.
representative_claim: The number of people required for any given task drops a lot.

frame_description: Smaller teams can produce more companies and products rather than simply eliminating all work.
representative_claim: We’ll get an explosion of entrepreneurship, an explosion of founders, and a very large number of very small teams.

frame_description: Pure software production becomes less scarce as agents can create code.
representative_claim: The thing I’m now thinking through is—is pure software dead?

frame_description: Agent infrastructure and interfaces become valuable complements to generated software.
representative_claim: Pretty soon every good SaaS company or hosting provider will have a CLI and API interface the models can use directly.

frame_description: Reusable software building blocks prevent agents from recreating infrastructure from scratch.
representative_claim: Another metaphor I’ve been using: anything that’s already been created that the models can reuse is like a token cache.

frame_description: Proprietary data and distribution may remain defensible when code is abundant.
representative_claim: Humans are the ones who generate surprise completely out of the data distribution, and they do it with intent—and intent matters for meaning.

frame_description: Real-world feedback loops and physical operations retain value beyond code generation.
representative_claim: A lot of the old function of people, lawyers, engineers, operations people, moves to verifying the stack and saying, “Yeah, this is roughly correct, I’ll roughly stand behind it, I’ll support you if it goes wrong.”

frame_description: Agentic methods accelerate hardware and engineering design.
representative_claim: If you’re designing a turbine blade—classically, a turbine blade starts cold, but when it runs it gets hot, so it gets bigger.

frame_description: Faster design shifts constraints toward manufacturing and industrial iteration.
representative_claim:
The job of the engineer being something where you just show up to work, you ship the output directly, and everything inside the company was—“how good is person A at shipping output B?”

And now what’s happening is, the way I’m judging you as an engineer is, “are you producing the factory that will produce multiplicative outputs B through Z?”

That’s a pretty significant change.

frame_description: Physical testing and empirical feedback remain necessary constraints.
representative_claim: Your exit criteria is that you’ve complied with all of these regulations.” There’s totally a world where we say the regulations are great — they’re like our test suite.

frame_description: Cheaper software makes vertically integrated industrial companies more feasible.
representative_claim: On the hardware side, this is a boon for all these little gadget companies and part companies that write really bad software because they can’t make great software.

frame_description: Factories and production systems accumulate proprietary operational knowledge.
representative_claim: We’ve instrumented our foundry as well as many other parts of the company in ways where, as these models get better, that should show up pretty immediately in things like the cell engineering we’re doing and the material science we’re developing.

frame_description: Regulation becomes a binding constraint when technical development accelerates.
representative_claim: When they start regulating frontier models and start regulating GPUs, that stops as well.

frame_description: Institutional permission and fragmented jurisdiction slow deployment.
representative_claim: The problem is that it’s really difficult for humans to deal with understanding and complying with this, and every time you have to exchange a letter with the government, you wait months.

frame_description: Manufacturing capacity becomes scarcer relative to generated designs.
representative_claim: It creates an opportunity to really shift the regulatory model.

frame_description: Safety and validation requirements constrain deployment in high-stakes domains.
representative_claim: You can always find a scary case — a vaccine, or a famous medical disaster — but the regulations spread everywhere, the tentacles are everywhere, and there are all these contradictory regulatory bodies.

frame_description: Responsibility and accountability remain human or institutional constraints.
representative_claim: I think that is the most important problem to solve in the regulatory state.

frame_description: Pure software may lose scarcity while reusable infrastructure becomes more valuable.
representative_claim:
They’re basically saying—“hey, if I can just generate software on demand, then I don’t have this disadvantage anymore against Silicon Valley.”

That’s not the only reason they’re doing open source.

frame_description: Broad access to AI can coexist with wider performance gaps between users.
representative_claim: I’m seeing this now with some of our junior software engineers on the team—what’s the next step in their career progression?

frame_description: Fewer people per task can coexist with more total firms and projects.
representative_claim: It found several quarters’ worth of security-research progress in a couple of days, for fourteen thousand dollars of tokens—months of red-teaming, entire teams of people.

frame_description: Faster technical execution can increase the relative burden of regulation and validation.
representative_claim: They themselves have to be compliant with conditions placed by host governments, but it means there can be many thousands more reviewers than in the US.

frame_description: Cheaper tokens can increase the volume of outputs that require architecture and verification.
representative_claim:
Look at all these tokens that I’m paying for.”

I’m curious if you guys have seen the same—how do you measure ROI?
